7. Why do you get sunburns?
8. How are transparent things see-through?
9. Is water wet?
10. Why is water transparent?
11. Why is the sky blue?
12. Why is the ocean blue?
13. If the sky is blue why is the sun yellow?
14. Why is driftwood often a shiny silver color?
15. How are LED’s different colors of light?
16. How do LED’s work?
17. If there is a trampoline as big as the earth and all 8 billion people on earth double bounced one person would that person exit the atmosphere?
18. How do batteries work?
19. How do solar panels work?

21. If heat rises how come the higher up you are the colder it is?
22. If the ENTIRE bottom of the ocean was electricity-proof how much electricity would it take in order to electrify the ENTIRE ocean.
23. If the earth was next to a different sun with a different light spectrum, would the colors we see daily be different? Would the leaves on trees be orange?
24. What would happen if every single tree variant was grafted into a single tree? how big would it be? How small of a fragment of every tree would it have to be in order to fit into a single tree?
25. How much energy would be required in order to boil the salt out of the entire ocean.

text: A local 1500 acre farm just sold their entire property to a data center in our small town. The whole city begged the family not to sell and allow the data center to move in. They ended up selling and leaving town the same night. The local 5 people on our town commission or something all got bought off and approved the data center’s construction. author: @gkrpgfan
text:
Okay so this is what happened for all those interested:

So Windows 11 requires the Trusted Platform Module to run. Most likely, it was enabled on the BIOS level then Windows 10 was upgraded to Windows 11.

However, the user must have unplugged the PC and left it unplugged for a couple days and, with the CMOS battery dead, those settings disappeared. Therefore, the system would no longer boot as TPM was now disabled again.

author: @neatfeatguy
text:
I still have two blu-ray readers on my PC. Helps having two drives for reading discs when I pickup a lot of movies/tv shows. I can rip two discs at once. 

My plex server utilizes the same case in this video, has 2 - 4TB HDDs (for backup) and 2 - 4TB SSDs as the main drives. Plus I have an 8TB HDD in my main computer that I back everything up to from the server. Plus a second 8TB HDD for another backup that I can just hotswap out of the server (front drives are hotswap drives on the case) that is kept outside of the computers.

I wouldn't have my server running with just 1 drive in it. You lose it, it's all gone. That's just silly.

author: @HeabSaysNo
text: PSA - that GPU isn't actually in the "wrong slot" - this is a z97 gaming 7 board, which is rated for x16/x8x8/ and x8 x4 x4 - - most MOBOs will reduce the speed on the secondary slot whenever the primary slot IS BEING USED - but if there's no card in the primary slot, the secondary is typically full pciex16.

author: @SalemTechsperts
text:
HOW I DID THIS:
Plug a FAT32-formatted USB drive into the PS3.
Go to Saved Data Utility (PS3), locate your Red Dead Redemption save, press Triangle, and select Copy to the USB.

Connect the USB to your PC.
Download and open Bruteforce Save Data.
Point the application to your PS3 save folder on the USB (typically starting with
BLUS or BLES).
Click Decrypt PFD to remove the encryption.

Download the Red Dead Redemption Save
Editor by XB36Hazard.
Open your decrypted PS3 save file within the editor.
Change the target platform to PC (if the
PC option is missing in your specific build of the editor, select PS4/Nintendo Switch, as the modern ports utilize the exact same file structure).
Save the newly converted file.
• Load the Save on PC
Open your PC save directory located at:
Documents|Rockstar Games\Red Dead
Redemption\Profiles\[Your Profile ID|\
Move your converted save file into this folder.
Launch the game and load the file.

My biggest issue was BruteForce not decrypting properly. I had to then download RPCS3 and a Red Dead Redemption iso, play it until it saved the file, download Apollo save tool homebrew, swap the RPCS3 save file with the one from my PS3, then decrypt it inside Apollo.

I DONT KNOW IF THERE ARE OTHER GAMES THAT CAN DO THIS
Don’t ask me for help cause I just followed directions and don’t know anything.
video_title: Modding My PS3 Was a Waste of Time! #tech #technology #shorts #pc

text: I recently had an issue with my S25 Ultra a few months ago where one mourning the screen just died. Tried a hard restart on the phone but got no display no matter what I did as if the entire phone died. Took it to a reputable repair shop who is a licensed dealer through Samsung and did a diagnostic on it and turned out that the entire Oled display had completely bricked itself for some reason. Thankfully my phone was still covered under its normal 1-year warranty and I didn't pay a penny for a new display or any labor. Still a very strange issue as I have never had a phone screen die on me randomly, especially on a $1,600 phone.

author: @trogdor2002
text:
These flight attendants and pilots are genuinely dumb as a sack of rocks. "If you turn bluetooth off it's not actually a credible threat..." 

"Oh no, every single passenger hasn't turned their bluetooth off in every device they have stuffed in all corners of their carryon bags, NOW it's a credible bomb threat!"

This is why I don't fly 121 carriers anymore, so much drama over stupid security theater. There is no way to explain this away other than the flight crew being untrained or having a power trip. If it was a real threat, immediate turnaround with no announcement and depressurize to keep people in their seats. If it's just a prankster transmitting a troll message over bluetooth, have police nab him at the destination airport. Stupid.

author: @lottalotto691
text:
1. Satellites track your location, plus other tools in the phone to estimate your location while driving.
2. Traffic data is pooled mostly from other people using the same gps app or apps using the same data broker. 
3. Trends are developed over time to best calculate routes, traffic, and timing.
4. Map data is mostly open source meaning there are many contributors from all around the world to fill in street names, businesses, events, etc.
5. Routes are calculated with weighted pathing algorithms like A* (a-star), and fine tuned based on trends, like popular routes and traffic.
video_title: How does the GPS work?

author: @ThatVitaminC
text:
I love these guys because sometimes they’re so simple and it’s endearing. 

Because I’m a nerd like this I’m gonna explain it real quick. Over the last like 20 years they’ve been mapping things out via satellite as well as taking photos like for Google Maps so you have that out there. Yes a lot of work went into the programming of it but what they do now is it’s really a surveillance situation where they watch certain streets at certain times and they know when it’s more busy when it’s less busy, of course there’s gonna be differences and some days are gonna be different than others but most of the time it’s pretty on point like this morning I put my GPS on to say I need to go to work. I said I was gonna be there in 37 minutes. I didn’t get there until 45 minutes later so sometimes it’s right sometimes it’s wrong.

text:
I remember during my computer sciences class in HS, the teacher was going over different bite sizes, and asking us how many were per classification, like "what is a Megabyte?" and "how many bytes or kilobytes is it?" Finally he stumped us by asking:
 "What is a Petabyte?"

No response, then, after the long pause I raised my hand and answered: 

"The byte that preys on all the younger smaller bytes?"

Whole classroom was floored with Laughter.

author: @FredB-m3p
text: Accurate heart rate (HR) monitoring is a problem area I have encountered with wearable fitness trackers. Neither wrist devices nor the Oura ring have been able to monitor my HR accurately when it exceeds 90 beats per minute (bpm). During HIIT workouts, for example, when my HR gets up into the 150s to 170s, both the wrist-worn Whoop’s and the Oura ring’s data get completely dirty and these devices show a HR fluctuating erratically between the 90s and 120s. (A Polar chest strap I have tracks HR with high accuracy, but I prefer not having to put on a chest strap during workouts.) When I reached out to Whoop shortly after I had started wearing it on my wrist, Whoop advised that this issue I was having (which apparently is not uncommon) almost always can be resolved by putting the Whoop on a bicep strap. Whoop sent me a free bicep strap, and the HR tracking became almost as accurate as it is with my Polar chest strap. I now wear the Whoop on my wrist at all times except during workouts, when I move it to a bicep strap. My concern about wrist-worn devices like the Fitbit would be that for people like me for whom wrist-worn trackers do not generate accurate HR data, there is not a bicep strap option—at least not yet.

author: @Sec7ionSevenRIGS
text:
Fantastic work by the entire GN team.

Honestly, when I saw the runtime, I thought there was no chance I'd watch all 3+ hours. Yet somehow I ended up watching every minute. That's a testament to both the research and the storytelling.

My biggest takeaway is that personal computing itself isn't collapsing — but DIY enthusiast computing may be losing its place as a mainstream consumer activity.

What concerns me most isn't just rising prices or shrinking product selection. It's the gradual erosion of ownership, control, repairability, and freedom of choice as the industry shifts toward AI, cloud services, subscriptions, and enterprise-scale customers.

In a strange way, DIY PCs may become the vinyl records of computing: more niche, more expensive, and pursued primarily by people who genuinely love the craft.

Thought-provoking, exceptionally researched, and well worth the time.
video_title: COLLAPSE of Personal Computing | Investigation Into the Destruction of Ownership

author: @ImmensityYT
text:
*The smartest part of DuoBell is that it still sounds exactly like a normal bike bell!*

That’s because the innovation was never about making a louder or futuristic sound.

Nearly 50% of headphones sold today include active noise cancellation. 

Researchers also reported a 24% rise in cyclist-pedestrian collisions linked to reduced environmental awareness from ANC headphone use.

So Škoda partnered with researchers at the University of Salford to study how modern noise-cancelling systems react to traditional bike bells.

They discovered a small “safety gap” between 750–780 Hz that many ANC systems struggle to fully block. 

DuoBell was engineered around that frequency, combined with an irregular striking pattern designed to make the sound harder for noise-cancelling algorithms to suppress in real time.

During testing, pedestrians wearing ANC headphones reportedly noticed the bell up to 22 meters earlier and gained around 5 extra seconds to react.

A simple mechanical bike bell quietly exposing the limits of modern technology is what makes this campaign so brilliant.
____


Subscribe (@ImmensityYT) for more stories about products, psychology, and marketing done right! ⚡❤️

text:
Man, my build ended up being near all white components by coincidence. I didn't plan to make it one at all. The case I bought (Bequiet Dark Base Pro 901) was only available in white. At that point, I decided to just get a white air cooler to go with it: Sudokoo SV700v. The only thing I didn't get in white was the Psu: Lian Li 1200w 80plat in black. Kinda want those white cables though. 
Would've loved a Black and red configuration but, this white build is very pretty to look at too.

text:
Thanks so much for watching! This restoration cost the client about $1300. The meter inserts were designed in Adobe illustrator and I went through 7 print shops to find one that could do this. It's 6 layers of ink to block out light. It took me about 3 and a half months from design to completed prints.

**AC DC used to be the song that this video ended on but youtube decided to block the video from being viewed after 1.5million views unless I removed the song so that's why it ends all funky

author: @yeeturmcbeetur8197
text:
Full disclosure: I didn’t have time to watch past 12:16. 

I’m what you would call a knowledgeable professional in the field of emissions. 

Even with the drone shots, it was difficult for me to see if there were any analyzers measuring emissions on those stacks. There should be some type of CEMS device or TDL on that stack. More commonly a CEMS but sometimes paired with a TDL. 

The most of your pollutants from this will be CO and NOx. 

The main thing I wanted to touch on is federal regulations (the EPA) require emissions permits to be pulled before coming up with this type of equipment. Thus the need for monitoring equipment such as a CEMS to make sure that the operator of said equipment does not go over their permitted amount (they have to pay fines if so).

So my question is, where are their permits?

author: @sf-jim8885
text: I've seen the reverse happen on a professional monitor, where the screen was blank, but if I bounced a flashlight off the screen, I could see a very dim picture, which meant that all the video circuits were working OK but the CFL backlight was out. With some simple troubleshooting I figured out that the CFL driver board was bad. These driver boards are fairly standard among many brands of TV's & monitors. I found the part number stenciled on the circuit board, and ordered an identical one online, and that fixed the problem. So, for $45 I fixed a professional monitor that it would have taken almost $4k to replace. My boss was very happy.

text: My name is Yoshikage Kira. I'm 33 years old. My house is in the northeast section of Morioh, where all the villas are, and I am not married. I work as an employee for the Kame Yu department stores, and I get home every day by 8 PM at the latest. I don't smoke, but I occasionally drink. I'm in bed by 11 PM, and make sure I get eight hours of sleep, no matter what. Just like a baby, I wake up without any fatigue or stress in the morning. I was told there were no issues at my last check-up. I'm trying to explain that I'm a person who wishes to live a very quiet life. I take care not to trouble myself with any enemies, like winning and losing, that would cause me to lose sleep at night. author: @DSedesse
text: I’ve worked in the electronic section at Costco for years. None of the products are B grade. In fact most of them even including small appliances, like vacuums, for example, typically have additional features that you wouldn’t get on that product somewhere else. If you buy a Sony Bravia 8 at Costco, it comes with an upgraded backlit remote, movie credits, and a 5 year Allstate warranty which covers physical damage on top of the typical hardware faults.

text: Any Costco price that ends with .97 indicates it is a clearance item.  Typically, those clearance items have a significant price reduction. author: @frollard
text:
The reason they do costco exclusive part numbers is so that nobody can price match them.

Everything sold at costco is on consignment - (also why they take returns for absurdly long)  - the manufacturer bids to get access to the sales floor of costco and they have to be bargain basement prices to survive on the floor. If it doesn't sell they just go to auction, the manufacturer doesn't get it back.  (by default, without a huge 'fee').

The manufacturers can't afford to have everyone selling at wholesale prices so they don't allow price matching.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @CaryGordon3k
text: I used to work for an electronics manufacturer that sold their products in Costco as well. At the time the only difference between the model sold at Costco and the standard model was the model number. The thing is that the warranty was doubled when sold through Costco, which was immediately adjusted for in our ticketing system when repair tickets were opened. When items couldn't be repaired Costco immediately swapped out for a replacement unit and our RMA was done through the Costco, super convenient.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @AmosAAnderson
text:
No, the products at Costco are not B-Line rates. Often the difference is that Costco can sell x number of products and so can do it cheaper, but to sell the same products at that much lower than MSRP is not fair to other retailers. They will sometimes change what comes with it, change the warranty, etc. but very often the model number change is just so they can say they're not selling it under MSRP because it's not the same as the product everyone else is selling. This is not a new practice and has been going on between Costco and Sam's Club for many years. I used to do customer service/tech support for several large television, camera, etc. companies and that was pretty much what we were told. I've never seen it be different and I buy Costco all the time.

Buying something at Costco is almost always and upgrade, not a downgrade.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @Durakken
text:
Costco prices are based on a different metric than the average store. Basically. There is this accounting that most stores do of their inventory where they say they can go through their inventory 3 times a year for example and then they negotiate with manufacturers based on that number. Most stores metrics say they'll only go through a few times, if I remember right 3-6. Costco pushes that they will go through their inventory something like 20-30 times a year. To do this requires that they price lower and work with manufactures, which they can because manufactures can then move more of their product.

This is not the case with Walmart though. They just have so much domination over the market that they demand lower prices when you see them.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @RapidNack
text:
As someone who works in the Consumer Packaged Goods industry and sells to Costco, I can tell you first-hand that they genuinely force companies to accept lower margins, while handing a majority of your own (the company's) margin to Costco. This is still seen as a good deal as Costco moves a lot of volume and it ends up still being profitable for the company. 

To top this off, sometimes companies will actually have very slightly different products that vary for Costco/club channels. A good example of this is the Sony carrying case. Sometimes though, the product itself will be worse.

Overall, Costco is always going to be a great deal for the customer, still a good deal for the company/seller, and even better for Costco

author: @jacobperez8921
text: 2:33 That exactly why goods present in retail stores like Marshalls or Burlington are much cheaper than in mall retail stores like Macys or JCPenny. They are products that don't meet the quality control certification to be sold at regual mall outlets but are good enough in quality to be sold at discount stores minus small inperfections from the factory such as loose threads or a small hole not properly sowed into.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @thevalleybc
text: Electronic items have a different model number when an identical item can be purchased elsewhere. This prevents price protection because the model numbers are different and also keeps outside returns from coming to the store. I learned this practice back in the day from a guy at A&b Sound up here in Canada.. that store would change something ever so slight and give it a different model number for the same reason.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @mrdarthdan
text: I had some issues with my Bose QC’s bought from Costco nearly 2 years ago and they returned them. Incredible return policy. I buy whatever I can at Costco due to their commitment to customer satisfaction.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @the_tig
text:
When it ends at 97 cents like the MB Air, it is on clearance.

Different product numbers but basically the same product is because of things like price matching policies manufacturers have with other retailers, to get around the MSRP of the 'regular' model other retailers have to sell at, etc.  it has nothing to do with 'b stock' or left overs with these electronics.

Now Costco does do old new stock with things like high end sunglasses.  They are a fraction of the original MSRP, but you are limited to the stock on hand at your local store, and what is specifically listed on their website for shipping to you.
video_title: Why is Costco Tech SO Cheap?

author: @mauricesanchez6804
text: The electronics they make there are usually made with fewer features than the ones you would find at full price in other retailers. It's still quality product, but depending on what it is at times you're getting a great deal, or you're just better off buying the original products directly from the companies. Someone who worked at Toshiba had told me that they would make TVs specifically for Costco, and it would be lesser quality compared to their top tier models. Not to say their quality is worse, just not the same.

author: @LogicalNiko
text: The biggest problem with early ammonia adsorption refrigeration is that they have to be constructed very well with heavy lines.  This is because high pressure heated ammonia is extremely flammable.  In fact early in industrial refrigeration explosions were a pretty big risk.  The other factor is the anhydrous ammonia if leaked would react with water to form ammonium hydroxide and could self ignite as well as cause suffocation in a closed room.

text: That Crosley Ice Bomb is basically how gas-fired refrigerators work(ed) in old campers and RVs. author: @WadeMurdock-m7u
text: The Icy Ball wasn’t really meant to rapidly force warm things cold; it was more for keeping modest food items cool, like butter, eggs, milk, or a small amount of meat. The manual also describes an optional “stabilizer” that the cold ball rests in, filled with glycerin or another odorless antifreeze solution plus water, which acts as a cold thermal buffer and increases the hours of refrigeration. So testing it by putting the bare cold ball in a box full of lukewarm canned sodas is a pretty harsh test. Drinks are mostly water and take a lot of heat removal to cool down, so that’s asking a lot from a century-old off-grid refrigerator.

author: @fireaza
text: The interesting thing about the Virtual Boy is that although it had a stereoscopic 3D display, the actual hardware wasn't capable of 3D polygonal graphics. The 3D effect was created by layering 2D sprites at different depths. This meant it would only ever be a primitive 3DS, even if you could wear it, there would be no point, since you'd be fixed to a 2D plane. At best, it would like mouse aiming in DOOM.

author: @RannonSi
text:
Thing is that, ignoring everything bad, I loved what the Google glasses could potentially have been. Like remind you who people you meet are (and why/how much you should care), what restaurants and what options you've tried (and your opinions), show you a pathway to the place you want to go to, so that you don't have to take out your phone all the time.
While yes, the marketing was bad, it was also a lot of other reasons, some have changed/lessened due to 'creeping normality', smartphones were a much newer thing and society has changed, a lot, when it comes to the expectation of privacy that people have (or are expected to have).

author: @joezegers
text:
There's a "big seven" when it comes to browsers right now.
Edge: If you're too lazy to install a browser on Windows.
Chrome: If you want a browser that is reliable.
Firefox: If you want a browser that is more secure than Chrome. It's also the Linux equivalent of Edge.
Safari: The MacOS equivalent of Edge.
Brave: If you want Chrome with more privacy.
Opera: If you think basic browsers are too mainstream.
Vivaldi: If you think Opera is *_still_* too mainsteam for you.

Another way to view this is:  Plumbing, Electrical, Structural.

Arteries (always preferable to veins) fall under plumbing, nerve bundles are electrical, and connective tissue like tendons are structural. None of em matter, tho, if you don't have a good understanding of WHERE they are and what is required (force wise) to damage each.

author: @Andrew-cm5tc
text: He probably doesn't know that lots of nerves, arteries, and veins usually travel together. Your axillary nerves (median, ulnar, and radial nerves) in your armpit are touching the axillary artery. The femoral nerve is right next to the femoral artery. I've also seen someone who got shot in the femoral artery and without the tourniquet, it bled like a geyser. It did not "retract" and stop bleeding.

this is not a good way of explaining timers and switches. timers are essentially just wounds that if not treated are going to kill you probably within minutes. switches are instant. lights out, dead. timers will have your body go into the different stages of shock while switches are things that your body cant even try to compensate for. think of like, getting shot in the head, or suffering catastrophic trauma to your heart.

author: @dillenbeck53531
text:
Good initial purchase cost and performance review.  My questions: what is the comparative cost after 1 screen and 1 keyboard repair, including turn around time for the repair? Costs to use with external monitor, keyboard, mouse, and external drive storage?  What if you discover you need more ram or better cpu? What if you realize taking calculus and chemistry notes are better input with a stylus, even if it is slightly laggy? (Add the cost of an iPad with stylus to see which option is cheaper.) 

Basically Apple is buying a disposable razor and Framework is buying a safety razor that takes standard razor blades. Sure the first time cost is way lower -but constant replacement costs at a low price will make the higher investment item lifetime costs far cheaper.

Of course, there is the whole software compatibility issue for students also. If you're taking engineering classes with CAD software that only runs on windows, then neither of these will do.

author: @QueeQuey
text:
Recent grad (Computer Science) with a Lenovo Yoga here, I actually loved the "tablet" layout but I didn't carry it around and hold it like a tablet like you show in this video. I got a pen to pair to my laptop and would fold the laptop in half, then set  it onto the desk. In that form factor it was just a notebook replacement for me and I wrote all of my notes that way. I know that "physically" writing your notes helps you remember them easier and anecdotally I think it helped, especially in classes that were harder like Algorithms or Operating Systems. Being able to write directly onto downloaded slides also helped a lot if there were a good amount of images for visual aids. I also used it a lot for quickly writing up some UML before actually using a tool to create the UML since it's a lot easier to just draw UML and figure things out before actually going into a tool specifically for that. I'm not an artist so I don't exactly need a fancy drawing tablet specifically made for that, having one built in was great, for actual artists I could see why they'd go with 2 separate devices like a Neo for their papers (etc) then an iPad or fancy Wacom for their actual art projects. 

Yeah it does smudge up the screen a little, but it's pretty easy to wipe off, so not a huge deal. If I magically had to start my degree over again I'd definitely choose the Framework here.

text: Crazy that you have even been in interviews with one of the founders of Framework and still make this awesome comparison. When framework launched some products I really wanted to buy, I have both mac and pc for gaming. Unfortunately, the RAM upgrade, and the different upgrades in general seem like a 'premium' price but not premium product, also upgradeability sounds nice in theory but in real world not so much. I have been thinking when was the last time I used USB A or HDMI and its been years. Most smart tvs have wifi connection to present, USB A was for USB sticks but now you have fast internet or sharing through wifi too and something similar with SD cards and cameras, I already have a dongle somewhere. I don't think Framework should price their laptops like that, or even their desktops, just too expensive for the quality they offer

author: @dawson6767
text:
I dont want to be the bearer of bad news but this actually doesnt hurt your battery life, ion-lithium batterys have a BMS that stop the flow of power to the battery at 100% the green light staying on doesnt use the battery just the power from the plug. If ion lithium batterys took charge after 100% they would overcharge and not be pretty. Hope this helps

Ps: to keep ion lithium batterys life longer charge when you reach 50% this is the recommened, you can play until its low anyway. But they recommened 20-50% to charge it to keep battery life the longest

author: @tracyg9721
text:
Thermal Pollution: The nodes use cold seawater for "free supercooling" of the intense AI chips. When that water is flushed back out, it’s significantly warmer. Dumping warm water into pristine deep-sea environments can disrupt local ecosystems, alter microscopic plankton blooms, or accidentally attract species that shouldn’t be there.  
 Acoustic Disturbance (Noise): Heavy waves driving massive internal turbines 24/7 means these structures will hum constantly. Sound travels incredibly fast and far underwater, which could potentially interfere with the echolocation and communication of whales, dolphins, and traveling marine mammals.  
 Electromagnetic Fields (EMF): Generating a megawatt of electricity inside a localized steel structure creates a distinct electromagnetic footprint. Certain migratory fish, sharks, and rays are hypersensitive to these fields, which could disorient their natural navigation.  
 Biofouling Solutions: Barnacles, algae, and mussels love attaching to steel structures (biofouling). To stop them from clogging the water intakes, companies often have to use specialized anti-fouling coatings or chemical treatments, which can slowly leach toxins into the surrounding water.

author: @bobalmond8257
text: These aircraft look like they do because form follows function. That is the best form of an item would be discovered on multiple planets because that form fulfills the function of the item the best. Of course, this is also influenced by the materials we can build something with. Obviously, if we have a super strong substance, we can make something much less thick that can hold up the same as something that is thicker made with a less durable substance.  As an example: If the aliens have access to super strong, super thin cables, then their cables will be much thinner but tougher than ours.

author: @LoganCableTech
text:
Current Cable tech here.

For the love of your parents and their parents and their parents dogs, please, PLEASE, do NOT DO THIS. 

Short: this allows signal ingress, causing signal to not be good and you sad.

Long form: this causes signal ingress, and most likely you will end up with a filter on your line as if it gets so bad enough, the neighbors will be affected and the maintenance team will be forced to put a filter on your line to stop it from affecting others around you. 
Cable signal is a lot like a hose, if you don’t have a solid connection, it will leak. And this is a cheap dollar tree hose connection that will be fine initially, but within a few minutes, it will be not great. 

Do yourself a favor, please have a technician come out, or, if you see one of us around, just ask for a proper jumper. We have the materials in our trucks to make one, and with experience comes a good cable. Give them the rough footage and we usually will be fine to make you one. Unless the techs a buttmunch, in which case they’ll probably just tell you “sorry, no” in which case just say “no problem” and walk away.
We don’t do it for random people often, but if it’s like a 2-4 foot jumper, we’re fine with it.

Have a good week, everyone.

author: @migidid
text:
1st-person camera view gives a massive visibility advantage over 3rd-person chase cams through corners and crests.
In 3rd person, the camera pivots behind the car, meaning the vehicle's own body literally blocks your view of the apex or the downward slope of a hill.
Moving the camera forward completely eliminates that blind spot. Because your viewpoint is right over the front axle, the camera shifts instantly the moment you steer, rather than waiting for the rear of the car to swing around. It gives you millimeter-precision for clipping curbs and lets you see through blind corners way earlier, even if you lose a bit of situational awareness of cars right next to your rear quarter panel.

author: @Derk91
text:
0:44 That's an LPN. It means it's been bought from Amazon, returned, and processed back into the system, and then resold on Amazon. Anything that gets returned and processed through an Amazon Relo site will have an LPN. Whether it gets resold as new, WHD (warehouse deals), unsellable (return to vendor), liquidation (wrapped batch on pallet usual for super cheap or at auction), donation, or destroy/recycle.

I work at a relo site.

author: @Kraaketaer
text:
As a non-apple user who has been quite critical of them throughout the years, I really like a lot of what they're doing with their more budget minded options these days. Unfortunately, their products never seem to fit the bill for me, as I tend to oscillate between cheap and high(ish)-end in a way that doesn't really align with what Apple offers. The MB Neo might change that though - it's the only Apple product that I have genuinely considered buying (with the halfway exception of the M4 Mac Mini), with the main reason for me not having done so being the display quality (I have a 14" OLED laptop, and while the Neo would be much smaller, lighter, and have vastly better battery life, I would *really* miss that display). 

But other than that? 
- I'm not a tablet user, but if I was to get one I'd want a good pen and display - but the Air starts at far too high a price for 128GB of storage, the Pro is just too expensive for an occasional use product (even if that display looks amazing), and the iPad just won't cut it.
- for earbuds, my CMF buds 2 deliver much better sound quality than the AirPods for half the price, and have (good!) ANC to boot, and if I want good audio quality I'll use my wired Sennheisers
- I find the health/fitness aspects of the Watch compelling, but beyond that and notifications i don't actually want a "smart" watch, and my basic, entry level Garmin does a lot of that, has a sunlight readable display, and week-long battery life for half the price. Charging my watch every day would drive me crazy.
- as for the iPhone ... Again I feel like the display is the major drawback. The accessory ecosystem is amazing, and the phones are by all accounts very good, but I'm not paying for a Pro, and giving up 120Hz would be very rough. I'm not all that happy with my Pixel 8 (honestly its battery life is disappointing, especially with performance being mediocre for its generation), but I'd still take it over an iPhone from the same generation (and changing phones before you have to is, at this point, just dumb).

And, crucially: once I start looking for Apple products that might to some level meet my wants and needs, the prices shoot up massively. An iPad air with acceptable storage adds a huge chunk to the price. Even the Pencil is wildly expensive. The watches just don't have acceptable battery life, and Macs other than the Neo and M4 Mini have the same issue of astronomical price increases once you add a few minor upgrades.

Seems to me like the value proposition really *only* exists at the very low end and with the base models - which is great if that's all you need, but for those of us who have worked in sales, we know what being set up for a high margin upsell looks like.

author: @Master-P-Adjacent
text:
When I was a dumb teen, my friends and I used to break into these and set the flashers off at 3 am. Then we’d sit in the edge of the woods smoking a blunt laughing while people slowed to 15 mph at 3 am, and laughing even harder when cops showed up trying to figure out who tampered with them after the 3rd time in a month. 

Damn we were stupid, stay in school kids.
video_title: School Zone Flasher Repair!

author: @MikeM-cz5ln
text: Those Eltec school clock controllers are the BEST. Never any issues with them. The clock is powered by a 1 farad super capacitor. Fully charged in 5 minutes. Holds the correct time for 2 days during a power outage. The on/off program times is permanently stored on an eprom. Oh, and did I mention how reliable they are? Love those controlles. "A" key press for (a)ttention followed by 2 digit function code and "B" key for enter. Menu screened onto the controller - no manual needed.
video_title: School Zone Flasher Repair!

author: @Coffe1Coffe
text:
Before people come here arguing about the prices, this is just my opinion on it. I don’t think Craighill’s products are overpriced for the level of design they offer. There are brands like Gucci selling items at much higher prices that, in my opinion, don’t even come close to the thought and craftsmanship this company puts into its work.

Like any luxury or premium brand, these products obviously aren’t everyday purchases for everyone, and that’s understandable. But personally, I think they’re beautifully made, and the attention to detail is exceptional.

I do understand the criticism about “creating solutions for problems that don’t exist,” but I think the whole point of this company, and their videos, is to showcase the design process and the amount of thought behind every object they create.

As Steve Jobs once said: “Design is not just what it looks like and feels like. Design is how it works.”

author: @Dimmers
text: I have developed my company's AI Phone Agent, and it makes sense that it can't/won't answer questions about shopping at Target or 1+1. It's sticking to its focus and not stepping outside that may be against brand or business guidelines. I personally think it handled okay-ish. That said, for my company's AI phone agent, we added rephrasing contextual awareness back to the user. For example, in one instance, we had someone testing the system to ask how to bake a cake. Our agent replied along the lines of "While I can't help you bake a cake I can help you with X". This way, the user feels understood while staying within scope and guiding the user back to its core focus.

author: @nietzschebietzsche
text: Since college, ive been meaning to write a program for ordering pizza through the terminal but still havent gotten around to it. Sometimes youre just locked in, you need to type  something like 'getpizza - y' to do the complete order so you can keep ballin without also starving to death. The idea would be that you could have many flags for toppings, crust, size quantities, vendors, card details, etc., but you could also set defaults. Like store your card details  and delivery info in a keychain or encrypted sqlite database so the no arg command call would order through preconfigured defaults. The actual ordering would just be done with selenium workflows. Thats the most tedious part because website changes could break it. Hopefully someone smarter than me could figure thisbout with agents that have like an ontological understanding of web interfaces so they can figure out how to interact with any sane UI to complete the order.

text: I bought the 5070ti version of this exact PC 2 months ago from Microcenter Phoenix. I have built my own PC's (and encouraged others to do so) my entire life , mainly for the savings. This is the first time I've ever bought prebuilt because the price is completely unbeatable. I literally would not be saving money building this PC myself.
video_title: This Prebuilt Gaming PC is Shockingly Affordable

author: @HappyRBOB
text: One of the big reasons SIs are able to come in so low in cost right now is that they are buying in bulk (economy of scale) combined with having an average 3 month or more lag between when they buy the parts on contract and when they get the final product to market which means the price increases on parts hit them at a delay. If/when the prices ever come back down that will become a bane for them as it will take months for them to bring their own prices down to match or they will have to eat that extra cost.
video_title: This Prebuilt Gaming PC is Shockingly Affordable

author: @neo-noirsean8871
text:
I was inspired by JTC's "Boring PC" build and discovered we have a Micro Center in Brooklyn. I went there just to price bundles and check out their 'build your own' services. I walked out with the G760 Gaming PC for much cheaper then I could have built myself ($1799) and with higher tier components!

I remain incredibly impressed at the quality of parts and performance. I've been playing Cyberpunk on psycho settings for over 2 weeks at 180 fps without any issues. 

One personal note of praise: Power Spec installed no bloatware on windows 11.
video_title: This Prebuilt Gaming PC is Shockingly Affordable

author: @vuduong173
text: 17:18 I find it better to have a thin NVMe heatspreader than a thick one, especially for one that is mounted underneath the GPU. The airflow from the GPU helped with cooling performance on the NVMe heatspreader. I only discovered this when I was using an ASUS Prime motherboard, which has a thin heatspreader, so it can be mounted underneath the GPU. I switched to an ROG motherboard, which has a humongous heatspreader for the NVMe, and it performs far worse than having a thin heatspreader mounted underneath the GPU.

author: @paulbaker1604
text: Very nice solution.  BTW, to avoid all the wasted 'poop' instead of changing colors within a layer, I put the svg on my object as a modifier and emboss it on top.  So all the white is just one print with one chance of color.   I had embedded some lettering on a circular base I made and it literally used twice as much filament as the plain base.  Embossing saves a LOT.

author: @BruceLeedar
text: I don't even own an OLED (aside from my phone), but I still feel that for serious TV viewing the luminance detail of per-pixel lighting is critical for any high end display experience. While blooming artifacts can be skillfully reduced, there is no way for conventional array dimming LCD displays to replicate the crisp high contrast image detail that an OLED can. You're not getting a real 4K presentation when dimming zones are used with a high contrast image, it's more like an interpolation of 4K with whatever the dimming zone resolution is (to say nothing of dimming lag that afflicts most TVs which causes dynamic inaccuracies in high motion scenes).

text: Thanks for this review. I'm also grateful for the center stand, because I've never understood the logic of big-screen TVs with legs positioned at the far edges, requiring you to have an equally large console if you aren't wall mounting the TV. Practical details like this can make a real difference to buyers. Blooming on this new Bravia 7 looks like it could be a problem, though, so I'll wait for other reviews to investigate this issue fully. author: @J.A.P_8950
text:
I still think Contrast & PQ EOTF is what most customers want.

For example OLED tech has the 3D Pop for any & all scenes, but only because of its capabilities to create more contrast by specifically being able to map Black between the ranges of: MinTML 0.00 - 0.015

If True RGB can only map MinTML down to 0.015 - then that pov scene pop & contrast will be significantly reduced - To counter that you can boost highlight PQ...but most average customers want the 3D Pop & Contrast in both low luminance scenes & high luminance scenes, especially for HDR10 (+) & Dolby Vision

author: @rzadigi
text:
If this video was meant to expose mass AI data center cancellations, it sure didn’t convince me. Picking and choosing old news and bearish commentary from the past two years is not persuasive to anyone actually paying attention. These companies are reporting massive growth and earnings quarter after quarter and it’s pretty much the only sector moving the world economy. I understand if people don’t like data centers and their impacts on communities, but that does not mean these companies are on the verge of collapse. What this piece failed to mention is that even if a data center is cancelled in one location, there are numerous backup locations ready to go. Hello Texas! 
There is huge money being made now for people who hold these stocks. If you aren’t currently in the stock market and don’t want the rich to become even richer, then I’d suggest doing a bit of research and buying some stocks. Stock growth like this is extremely rare, even more so when it’s actually supported by strong financials. I’m kicking myself now for being lazy and missing out on last year’s massive gains. This year could be even greater.

author: @codyott1982
text:
When I was much younger, we used to have these green metal boxes. They were transformers and stuff for the electric grid. 

You don't see those boxes anymore. Even though they were very hard to get into, tweakers would always rob them anyway. 

Eventually the electric company got tired of having them robbed, and in response, the stuff is now all on the pole, and the boxes are gone. 

Total value varied, but it was never $100k or more. In my area, there was only a couple grand in them, and it was hard to sell, so they took the copper, and made a couple hundred on a couple thousand worth in stuff. 

This....far more money in it.

author: @greenlavalamp
text:
A host of one of these would be compensated to some degree, but it will put strain on the electric distribution system in the neighborhood. As strains on that system increase from these or like, air conditioners etc, the local utility will need to spend money to upgrade the distribution system, meaning those who aren't being compensated for one of these will have to bear the cost of their additional load. So even if you don't get one of these, your power bill can increase if a few neighbors get one. 

Distributing data centers around isn't any better than setting up behemoth ones like the one folks in north Utah are trying to fight now. Our costs will go up, and most states are failing to setup regulation to protect their citizens.
video_title: NVIDIA Partner Wants to Put Mini Data Centers in Your Yard

author: @tranka6112
text: Many pointed it out - typical house load, where the circuit breakers trip is 40A for 240V and 80A for 120V, or even half of that, depends on the house line. The CB has to trip after 59minutes of 1.5x nominal current. The 200A is a typical small block transformer nominal, not a single home line. That's one thing. The other thing is, that gas plants specifically are powered up on a daily basis for about 2 hours - for the evening come home time, and the morning get up time. In england for example, tea time in London needs an extra power plant turned on for just this 1-2 hour tea time. So what is described as 99% of the time ideling never happens, because on demand power plants always turn on or off several times per day. The grid developer who designed this, is the typical uninformed morron, who works in this business nowadays. Believe me, I work in this business, and I am a morron :D))

author: @HappyQuailsLC
text: To say that someone isn't using all of their 200 A household capacity is not to say you aren't using all of what you are paying for. It just means you have a physical limit on how much power any one residence can connect to the system in order to use in total, at one time. And 200A is a LOT of power! A full sized 40 foot motor coach connects at 50A, a Class C at 30A, ea. A small house can have a 75 A service just fine. This service sharing deal means they are making you pay their bill and that could easily more than double your it and good luck collecting on that in full and in time if they go under. In fact I highly doubt they will pay you in time to pay their bill on time in the first place. If not, you are becoming their financier as well. All of which sounds highly risky.

author: @InfansWatch
text: This one-bowl recipe yields rich, fudgy brownies with a perfectly crackly top.IngredientsUnsalted butter: 1 cup (226g), melted and slightly cooledGranulated white sugar: 2 cups (400g)Unsweetened cocoa powder: ¾ cup (75g)Large eggs: 3, at room temperatureVanilla extract: 1 tablespoonAll-purpose flour: 1 cup (120g)Salt: 1 teaspoonSemisweet chocolate chips: 1 ½ cups (270g)Step-by-Step InstructionsPrep the oven and pan: Preheat your oven to 350°F (175°C). Lightly grease an 8×8-inch metal baking pan and line it with parchment paper for easy removal.Whisk the base: In a large bowl, combine the melted butter, granulated sugar, and cocoa powder. Whisk vigorously for 30 seconds. This dissolves some of the sugar, which is the secret to getting that shiny, crackly top.Add wet ingredients: Whisk in the eggs, vanilla extract, and salt until the mixture is smooth and fully incorporated.Fold in dry ingredients: Add the flour and chocolate chips to the bowl. Switch to a rubber spatula and gently fold the mixture together just until the dry flour disappears. Do not overmix, or the brownies will become tough and cakey.Bake: Spread the thick batter evenly into your prepared pan. Bake for 35 to 40 minutes.Test for doneness: Insert a toothpick into the center. For ultra-fudgy brownies, it should come out with a few moist crumbs or a slight smear of batter, not completely clean.Cool and slice: Let the brownies cool completely in the pan on a wire rack before lifting them out and slicing into squares. They will firm up as they cool.
